text:
DEPARTMENT OF NEUROLOGY — ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC LABORATORY
ELECTROMYOGRAPHY AND NERVE CONDUCTION STUDY REPORT

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PATIENT: Marcus T. Delgado
DATE OF BIRTH: 1965
SEX: Male
MRN: [REDACTED]
REFERRING PHYSICIAN: Medical Oncology Service
EXAMINING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Neurologist, Electrodiagnostics Faculty]
INDICATION: Evaluation of bilateral upper and lower extremity numbness, tingling, and weakness in the setting of chemotherapy exposure (oxaliplatin); rule out oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy; characterize severity and pattern.
STUDY TYPE: Electromyography (EMG) and Nerve Conduction Study (NCS) — bilateral upper and lower extremities
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

CLINICAL HISTORY:
Mr. Delgado is a 58-year-old Hispanic male with a known diagnosis of Stage IV adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases and pulmonary metastases, currently managed by the medical oncology service. He received 10 cycles of FOLFOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 for cycles 1–7, then reduced to 75 mg/m2 for cycles 8–10) in combination with bevacizumab as part of first-line palliative systemic therapy. Oxaliplatin was discontinued after cycle 10 due to development of grade 3 cumulative peripheral sensory neuropathy (CTCAE v5.0), at an estimated cumulative oxaliplatin dose of approximately 760 mg/m2. He subsequently completed cycles 11–12 as modified FOLFOX without oxaliplatin (5-FU/leucovorin/bevacizumab).

The patient reports a progressive course of bilateral distal extremity sensory symptoms beginning approximately 6 weeks into treatment as intermittent cold-induced dysesthesias in fingertips and toes, evolving to persistent numbness and tingling, and ultimately culminating in numbness involving bilateral feet to mid-calf level and bilateral hands to the wrists. He reports difficulty walking on uneven surfaces, three episodes of dropping objects, and difficulty with fine motor tasks including buttoning shirts and writing. He is currently receiving gabapentin 300 mg three times daily for symptom management, with partial relief. He denies focal weakness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, neck pain, or back pain. He denies alcohol use. He has well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus (on metformin) and mild hypertension. No family history of neuropathy or hereditary neuromuscular disease.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
NERVE CONDUCTION STUDIES
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

SENSORY NERVE CONDUCTION STUDIES:

Right Sural Nerve:
  Onset Latency: 3.9 ms (normal <4.4 ms)
  Peak Latency: 4.8 ms
  Amplitude (SNAP): 4.2 µV [REDUCED — normal ≥6 µV]
  Conduction Velocity: 43.1 m/s (normal ≥40 m/s)
  Interpretation: Reduced SNAP amplitude with preserved conduction velocity — axonal pattern

Left Sural Nerve:
  Onset Latency: 4.0 ms
  Peak Latency: 4.9 ms
  Amplitude (SNAP): 3.8 µV [REDUCED — normal ≥6 µV]
  Conduction Velocity: 42.4 m/s
  Interpretation: Reduced SNAP amplitude with preserved conduction velocity — axonal pattern

Right Superficial Peroneal Nerve:
  Amplitude (SNAP): 3.6 µV [REDUCED — normal ≥6 µV]
  Conduction Velocity: 42.8 m/s
  Interpretation: Reduced amplitude, axonal pattern

Left Superficial Peroneal Nerve:
  Amplitude (SNAP): 3.1 µV [REDUCED — normal ≥6 µV]
  Conduction Velocity: 41.9 m/s
  Interpretation: Reduced amplitude, axonal pattern

Right Median Nerve (sensory — digit II to wrist):
  Onset Latency: 3.2 ms (normal <3.5 ms)
  Amplitude (SNAP): 11.4 µV [mildly reduced — normal ≥15 µV]
  Conduction Velocity: 51.3 m/s
  Interpretation: Mild amplitude reduction, no significant slowing; distal axonal involvement

Left Median Nerve (sensory):
  Onset Latency: 3.3 ms
  Amplitude (SNAP): 10.8 µV [mildly reduced — normal ≥15 µV]
  Conduction Velocity: 50.7 m/s
  Interpretation: Mild amplitude reduction; distal axonal involvement

Right Ulnar Nerve (sensory — digit V to wrist):
  Onset Latency: 3.0 ms
  Amplitude (SNAP): 9.7 µV [borderline reduced — normal ≥10 µV]
  Conduction Velocity: 52.0 m/s

Left Ulnar Nerve (sensory):
  Onset Latency: 3.1 ms
  Amplitude (SNAP): 9.2 µV [borderline reduced]
  Conduction Velocity: 51.5 m/s

Right Radial Nerve (sensory — superficial branch):
  Amplitude (SNAP): 16.1 µV (normal)
  Conduction Velocity: 53.4 m/s (normal)

Sensory NCS Summary: Symmetric, length-dependent reduction in sensory nerve action potential amplitudes affecting the lower extremities (sural, superficial peroneal) more prominently than the upper extremities (median, ulnar), with relative sparing of radial sensory responses. Conduction velocities are mildly reduced or within the low-normal range, without significant demyelinating slowing. This pattern is consistent with a primarily axonal, length-dependent sensorimotor neuropathy, affecting the longest nerves first.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MOTOR NERVE CONDUCTION STUDIES:

Right Peroneal Nerve (extensor digitorum brevis recording; stimulation at ankle and fibular head):
  Distal Latency (ankle): 4.8 ms (normal <6.5 ms)
  Amplitude (CMAP): 3.2 mV [REDUCED — normal ≥2 mV; mildly reduced for age]
  Conduction Velocity (ankle to fibular head): 40.1 m/s [mildly reduced — normal ≥40 m/s; borderline]
  F-wave Latency: 53.4 ms (mildly prolonged; upper limit of normal ~52 ms)

Left Peroneal Nerve:
  Distal Latency (ankle): 5.0 ms
  Amplitude (CMAP): 2.9 mV [mildly reduced]
  Conduction Velocity: 39.6 m/s [mildly reduced]
  F-wave Latency: 54.1 ms (mildly prolonged)

Right Tibial Nerve (abductor hallucis recording):
  Distal Latency: 5.2 ms (normal <5.8 ms)
  Amplitude (CMAP): 6.1 mV (low-normal)
  Conduction Velocity: 41.8 m/s (low-normal)
  F-wave Latency: 55.9 ms (mildly prolonged)

Left Tibial Nerve:
  Distal Latency: 5.4 ms
  Amplitude (CMAP): 5.7 mV (low-normal)
  Conduction Velocity: 41.2 m/s (low-normal)

Right Median Nerve (motor — abductor pollicis brevis recording):
  Distal Latency: 3.8 ms (normal <4.4 ms)
  Amplitude (CMAP): 9.8 mV (normal)
  Conduction Velocity: 54.3 m/s (normal)
  F-wave Latency: 28.1 ms (normal)

Left Median Nerve (motor):
  Distal Latency: 3.9 ms
  Amplitude (CMAP): 9.4 mV (normal)
  Conduction Velocity: 53.8 m/s (normal)

Right Ulnar Nerve (motor — abductor digiti minimi recording; stimulation at wrist and below elbow):
  Distal Latency: 3.2 ms (normal)
  Amplitude (CMAP): 9.1 mV (normal)
  Conduction Velocity (wrist to below elbow): 55.2 m/s (normal)
  No conduction block or focal slowing across the elbow segment.

Motor NCS Summary: Mild reduction in peroneal and tibial CMAP amplitudes with borderline slowing of conduction velocities in the lower extremities, consistent with axonal motor involvement superimposed on predominantly sensory neuropathy. Upper extremity motor studies are normal. F-wave latencies mildly prolonged in lower extremities, consistent with distal axonopathy. No evidence of segmental demyelination, conduction block, or significant focal nerve entrapment.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ELECTROMYOGRAPHY (NEEDLE EMG)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Muscles sampled and findings:

  Right Tibialis Anterior:
    Insertional Activity: Mildly increased
    Spontaneous Activity: Rare fibrillation potentials (+1)
    Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP) Morphology: Mildly polyphasic, slightly increased duration
    Recruitment: Mildly reduced

  Right Peroneus Longus:
    Insertional Activity: Mildly increased
    Spontaneous Activity: Occasional fibrillation potentials (+1)
    MUAP Morphology: Mildly polyphasic
    Recruitment: Mildly reduced

  Right Gastrocnemius (medial head):
    Insertional Activity: Slightly increased
    Spontaneous Activity: Rare fibrillation potentials (+1)
    MUAP Morphology: Mildly polyphasic, increased duration
    Recruitment: Mild reduction

  Right Vastus Lateralis:
    Insertional Activity: Normal
    Spontaneous Activity: None
    MUAP Morphology: Normal
    Recruitment: Normal

  Right Gluteus Medius:
    Insertional Activity: Normal
    Spontaneous Activity: None
    MUAP Morphology: Normal
    Recruitment: Normal

  Right L4–L5 Paraspinal Muscles:
    Insertional Activity: Normal
    Spontaneous Activity: None
    Interpretation: No evidence of radiculopathy at lumbar levels

  Right First Dorsal Interosseous (hand):
    Insertional Activity: Mildly increased
    Spontaneous Activity: Rare fibrillation potentials (+1)
    MUAP Morphology: Mildly polyphasic
    Recruitment: Normal

  Right Abductor Pollicis Brevis:
    Insertional Activity: Normal
    Spontaneous Activity: None
    MUAP Morphology: Normal
    Recruitment: Normal

  Right Biceps Brachii:
    Insertional Activity: Normal
    Spontaneous Activity: None
    MUAP Morphology: Normal
    Recruitment: Normal

  Right C7–C8 Cervical Paraspinal Muscles:
    Insertional Activity: Normal
    Spontaneous Activity: None
    Interpretation: No evidence of cervical radiculopathy

EMG Summary: Mild active denervation (rare fibrillation potentials) with mild chronic neurogenic changes (polyphasic, increased-duration MUAPs) in distal lower extremity muscles and mildly in distal upper extremity intrinsic hand muscles. Paraspinal muscles normal bilaterally — no evidence of lumbosacral or cervical radiculopathy. No myopathic features. Pattern is consistent with length-dependent axonal sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LATE RESPONSES
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

F-Waves: As noted above — mildly prolonged in bilateral lower extremities (peroneal, tibial); upper extremity F-waves (median, ulnar) within normal limits.

H-Reflex (right soleus recording, tibial nerve stimulation at popliteal fossa):
  H-reflex absent bilaterally (right and left).
  Interpretation: Consistent with distal sensory axon dysfunction affecting the reflex arc; alternative consideration of diabetes-related contribution cannot be fully excluded.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DIAGNOSTIC SUMMARY AND INTERPRETATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The electrodiagnostic findings in Mr. Delgado reveal a SENSORIMOTOR PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY with the following characteristics:

1. DISTRIBUTION: Symmetric, length-dependent (stocking-glove distribution), affecting bilateral lower extremities more severely than upper extremities. Consistent with a "dying-back" axonopathy.

2. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: PREDOMINANTLY AXONAL pattern — characterized by reduced SNAP and CMAP amplitudes with relative preservation of conduction velocities. Mild conduction velocity slowing in lower extremities is proportional to loss of large myelinated fiber density (secondary demyelination from axon loss), rather than primary demyelination. No evidence of segmental demyelination, conduction block, or temporal dispersion.

3. SENSORY vs. MOTOR: Predominantly sensory, with modest motor involvement limited to distal lower extremity muscles. Upper extremity motor studies normal.

4. ACTIVE vs. CHRONIC: Active denervation (fibrillation potentials) in distal lower extremity and intrinsic hand muscles indicates ongoing axonal injury or incomplete recovery. Chronic neurogenic changes (polyphasic, increased-duration MUAPs) indicate subacute-to-chronic course.

5. ABSENT H-REFLEXES: Indicates significant large-fiber sensory dysfunction in the distal lower extremities.

6. DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS CONSIDERATION: The electrodiagnostic pattern is most consistent with oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy (cumulative dose approximately 760 mg/m2), which characteristically produces a predominantly sensory, length-dependent axonal neuropathy targeting dorsal root ganglion (DRG) neurons. Although the patient has a known diagnosis of type 2 diabetes mellitus, the severity and distribution of electrodiagnostic abnormalities exceed what would be expected for mild diabetic polyneuropathy alone, and the clinical timeline correlates directly with oxaliplatin exposure. The contribution of diabetic polyneuropathy to the overall picture cannot be excluded and may have lowered the threshold for oxaliplatin neuropathy at this cumulative dose. No features of hereditary neuropathy (no family history; no high-arched feet; no demyelinating pattern).

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CONCLUSION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC CONCLUSION: SENSORIMOTOR PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY, PREDOMINANTLY SENSORY, LENGTH-DEPENDENT, AXONAL PATTERN, AFFECTING BILATERAL UPPER AND LOWER EXTREMITIES.

The findings are consistent with OXALIPLATIN-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY in a patient with known exposure to cumulative high-dose oxaliplatin (approximately 760 mg/m2) for colorectal cancer treatment. The pattern reflects DRG neuronopathy/axonopathy characteristic of platinum compound neurotoxicity.

SEVERITY CLASSIFICATION: Moderate-to-severe electrodiagnostic changes in lower extremities; mild changes in upper extremities.

PROGNOSTIC NOTE: Based on existing literature regarding oxaliplatin-induced neuropathy of this severity (corresponding to clinical grade 3 per CTCAE v5.0), partial recovery is anticipated following cessation of oxaliplatin. However, complete resolution is uncertain, particularly given the severity at time of oxaliplatin discontinuation. Recovery, when it occurs, typically follows a slow time course of months to years, with sensory symptoms generally improving more than motor deficits. The presence of mild diabetic neuropathy as a potential contributing comorbidity may affect the extent of recovery.

ANCILLARY STUDIES:
  - Serum B12, folate, TSH, SPEP with immunofixation (to exclude other treatable causes): Results pending at time of this report.
  - Genetic testing for hereditary neuropathy (e.g., PMP22 duplication/deletion): Not indicated given clinical picture; deferred unless recovery is unexpectedly poor.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SIGNATURE
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Electrodiagnosis performed and interpreted by: [Attending Neurologist, Electrodiagnostics Division, Department of Neurology]
Report dictated and signed electronically.
Copies distributed to: Medical Oncology Service (referring provider), Primary Care Physician.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
[END OF REPORT]
